coredata.dc:title: Green fluorescent carbon dots from chitosan as selective and sensitive “off-on” probes for nitrite and “on-off-on” probes for enrofloxacin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiyao
Qi, Quan
Sun, Lili
Guo, Xiangjun
Zhang, Hongmei
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, green emission fluorescence carbon dots (G-CDs) were prepared using chitosan and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the prepared G-CDs was quenched by Nitrite (NO2 -) with excellent selectivity and sensitivity due to internal filtration effect (IFE) Therefore, G-CDs was applied to monitor NO2 - as an “on-off” fluorescence probe and obtained a wide linear range (1–300 μM) with limit of detection (LOD) 1.00 μM. Another hand, the fluorescence of G-CDs was also quenched by Au3+ due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect caused by the in-situ formed Au nanoparticles (AuNPs). Interestingly, the addition of enrofloxacin (Enr) to G-CDs/AuNPs system led to the aggregation of AuNPs with partly lager size G-CDs, and the quenched fluorescence of the G-CDs/AuNPs system was restored, accompanying with visible fluorescence color change from green to blue. The “on-off-on” detection method through the constructed G-CDs/AuNPs system to Enr obtained good effects, such as high selectivity, sensitivity, visibility, relatively wide linear range (1–50 mg/L) and low LOD (0.04 mg/L). Additionally, the prepared G-CDs could be used for the detection of NO2 - and Enr in actual water samples, proving it is an excellent candidate for environmental detection of NO2 - and Enr.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chitosan
Fluorescence sensor
NO2 - detection
Enrofloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of NO2 - and Enr by G-CDs
The “on-off” fluorescent probe of G-CDs for detection of NO2 -
The “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for detection of Enr

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) HRTEM image, (C) particle size distribution histogram, (D) FT-IR, and (E) XRD spectrum of G-CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of G-CDs. (B-D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s of G-CDs, respectively.

(A) Excitation, emission and absorbance spectra of G-CDs. Inset: photographs of G-CDs under daylight and UV light of 365 nm. (B) The emission spectra of G-CDs excited with different wavelengths. (C) The CIE coordinates of G-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs after adding different anion. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of G-CDs with different anion ions. (C) Photographs under 365 nm UV lamp after adding different anion ions to G-CDs. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs to different concentrations of NO2 -.
(A) Fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs and G-CDs+NO2 -. (B) FT-IR of G-CDs and G-CDs+NO2 -. (C) UV absorption of G-CDs+NO2 - and excitation, emission spectra of G-CDs. (D) Reaction simulation diagram.

(A) Relative fluorescence intensity of G-CDs adding different metal ions at excitation wavelength 390 nm. (B) Photographs after adding different metal ions to G-CDs. (C) TEM image of G-CDs+Au3+. (D)XRD of G-CDs+Au3+. (E) UV absorption of G-CDs +Au3+ and excitation, emission spectra of G-CDs. Inset: photographs of G-CDs+Au3+. (F) Fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs and G-CDs+Au3+.
(A) Selectivity of G-CDs+Au3+ to Enr. (B) FL emission spectra of G-CDs and G-CDs+Au3++Enr. (C) Pictures of G-CDs, G-CDs+Enr, G-CDs+Au3+ and G-CDs+Au3++Enr. (D) TEM after adding Enr to G-CDs/Au3+.
Illustration of synthesis and detection principle of fluorescent G-CDs.
Comparison of G-CDs as a fluorescence sensor for NO2 − detection with other reported methods.
Determination of NO2 − in tap water samples.
Comparison of G-CDs/AuNPs as a fluorescence sensor for Enr detection with other reported methods.
Determination of Enr in tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Green fluorescent carbon dots from chitosan as selective and sensitive “off-on” probes for nitrite and “on-off-on” probes for enrofloxacin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, green emission fluorescence carbon dots (G-CDs) were prepared using chitosan and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the prepared G-CDs was quenched by Nitrite (NO2 -) with excellent selectivity and sensitivity due to internal filtration effect (IFE) Therefore, G-CDs was applied to monitor NO2 - as an “on-off” fluorescence probe and obtained a wide linear range (1–300 μM) with limit of detection (LOD) 1.00 μM. Another hand, the fluorescence of G-CDs was also quenched by Au3+ due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect caused by the in-situ formed Au nanoparticles (AuNPs). Interestingly, the addition of enrofloxacin (Enr) to G-CDs/AuNPs system led to the aggregation of AuNPs with partly lager size G-CDs, and the quenched fluorescence of the G-CDs/AuNPs system was restored, accompanying with visible fluorescence color change from green to blue. The “on-off-on” detection method through the constructed G-CDs/AuNPs system to Enr obtained good effects, such as high selectivity, sensitivity, visibility, relatively wide linear range (1–50 mg/L) and low LOD (0.04 mg/L). Additionally, the prepared G-CDs could be used for the detection of NO2 - and Enr in actual water samples, proving it is an excellent candidate for environmental detection of NO2 - and Enr.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon material, have attracted great interest due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, good water solubility, biocompatibility, simple and green synthesis conditions, and extensive sources of precursor materials [1–3]. In recent years, CDs have broad applications in biological imaging [4], photocatalysis [5], drug delivery [6] and fluorescence sensing, especially as high-efficiency fluorescent probes to monitor ions, biomolecules, proteins and so on [7,8]. For example, Zhou et al. employed bright red-emitting P, Br co-doped carbon dots as "OFF-ON" fluorescent probes for Cu2+ and L-cysteine detection [9]. Supchocksoonthorn et al. developed a novel sensor based on fluorescent carbon dots for fast, selective and sensitive detection of pyrimethamine[10]. Although many kinds of CDs have been synthesized using a variety of different raw materials, such as orange juice [11], malic acid [12], Ginkgo biloba [13], and cystine [14], most of the reported CDs emit blue light, as well as short excitation wavelength. According to the previous reports, excitation wavelength of CDs has an important relationship with the nitrogen and oxygen content and ratio, and nitrogen doping is an important strategy to change the surface functional groups of CDs and efficiently tune the surface defects, which not only facilitates the interaction between CDs and target substance, but also effectively improves the test sensitivity, as well as broadens the application of CDs in detection and sensing field.

NO2 - is the most common nitrogen-containing compound in nature and widely exists in various environments, especially in food [15]. However, NO2 - is a highly toxic substance. It can oxidize low-iron hemoglobin in the blood to methemoglobin, thereby making hemoglobin lose its ability to carry and transport oxygen. 0.2–0.5 g intake may be poison to adults and 3 g may be fatal [16]. According to the guidelines of the World Health Organization, the maximum limit of NO2 - in drinking water is< 65 μM. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the maximum pollutant level (MCL) of NO2 - as 1 ppm (or 21.7 μM) [17]. On the other hand, Enrofloxacin (Enr) is a broad-spectrum veterinary antibiotic, which is widely used in animal husbandry and fish farming. However, excessive use will lead to its accumulation in the human body, affect human body's tolerance to bacteria, and improve bacterial drug resistance [18]. Although multiple detection methods for NO2 - and Enr have been reported, such as spectrophotometry [19], chromatography [20], colorimetry [21], liquid chromatography and thin-layer chromatography etc [22,23], these methods are all limited by expensive instruments, complicated operation methods or long detection time. Besides, these methods require complicated pretreatment of samples and have limited capability for real-time detection. Thus, this paper focus on constructing a portable, rapid, sensitive, and effective fluorescence method for quantitative detection of NO2 - and Enr.

So far, some fluorescence sensors based on CDs had been developed for the detection of NO2 -. Karali et al. [24] used Tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane and urea to synthesize highly fluorescent N-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs) to detect NO2 -. Although the linear range was wide (15–1150 μM), the detection limit (LOD) is relatively high (13.5 μM). Wu et al. [25] synthesized the sensitive and selective colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout sensor with a lower detection limit (2.5 μM). However, the detection method needs complicated process. Guo et al. [26] adopted N-CDs-Cu2+ to detect Enr through fluorescence method with lower detection limit (0.16 mg/L), while its linear relationship was relatively limited. To the best of our knowledge, although many fluorescence sensors have been developed to detect NO2 - or Enr, there are few published reports on integrating two detection targets into one CD-based fluorescent sensor.

Chitosan is a partial deacetylated product of chitin, which mainly derived from crustacean shells. It is a favorable material for preparing the nitrogen doping CDs due to its hydroxyl and amino, which can be modified on the surface of CDs during carbonization. It is worth pointing that our group have reported the preparation of chitosan-derived CDs [27]. But the prepared CDs mainly emitted blue fluorescence. In this work, green fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots (G-CDs) was prepared by simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization with chitosan and p-phenylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen source. Remarkably, the prepared G-CDs revealed strong green fluorescence with good water solubility and nitrogen content of 9.63%. Afterward, we fabricated a simple and rapid fluorescence “on-off” sensor for the selective detection of NO2 -. More interestingly, G-CDs shows a selective quenching behavior to Au3+ (turn-off), while the quenched fluorescence of G-CDs/Au3+ system was effectively restored (turn-on) by Enr. Based on this, we innovatively constructed an “on-off-on” fluorescence sensor for determination of Enr with high sensitivity and selectivity. It is worth noting that the restored fluorescence changed from green fluorescence to blue fluorescence ( Scheme. 1).

P-phenylenediamine, chitosan was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the reagents for metal cation solution, anion solution, drugs, and amino acid solution were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Deionized water is used throughout the experiment. Pymetrozine (Pym), Kasugamycin (Kas), Oscalid (Osc), Carbendazim (Car), Enrofloxacin (Enr), Procymidone (Pro), Pyrethroid (Pyr), Cyromazine (Cyr), Lime Sulfur Mixture (LSM), Phoxim (Pho), Flonicamid (Flo), Isoprocarb (Iso) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed using JEM-1200 EX (Japan, JEOL) transmission electron microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was displayed with a DX-2700 X-ray diffractometer, and the measurement was performed with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 0°/5–90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a spectrometer (NICO-LET5700) by the KBr tablet method, with a scanning range of 400–4000 cm-1 and a resolution of 4 cm-1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence (FL) measurement was performed on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence decay curve was performed with Edinburgh FLS980 at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

Using chitosan and p-phenylenediamine as carbon source and nitrogen source, G-CDs were synthesized in one step by a hydrothermal method. Typically, mixed 10 mL of 0.5% chitosan (dissolved in 1% acetic acid) solution and 0.02 g of p-phenylenediamine uniformly. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 20 mL reactor and heated at 220 °C for 18 h in a muffle furnace. After completion of the hydrothermal carbonization process, a brown-black dispersion was obtained, which was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through a filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm. Finally, the obtained pale-yellow transparent liquid was named G-CDs and stored at 4 °C for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 - and Enr by G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared G-CDs was diluted 40 times with deionized water and different anions (NO2 -, HCO3 -, Br-, S2-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, SiO4 2-, MnO4 -, PO4 3-, HSO4 -, I-, Cl-, CO3 2-) were added, so that the final concentration of anions was 300 μM. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength 390 nm to analyze the selectivity of G-CDs to NO2 -. Besides, different concentrations of NO2 - (0–300 μM) were detected to analyze the sensitivity and linear relationship. The interference experiment was carried out under the coexistence condition of interfering substance and NO2 - with 1:1 concentration (300 μM). Finally, the real water samples were analyzed at three arbitrary concentration points in the linear equation.

At the same method, different metal ions (Au3+, Ag+, Hg+, Fe3+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Co2+) were added to the G-CDs solution and further recorded the fluorescence emission spectra at the excitation wavelength 390 nm. The selectively fluorescence quenching response of G-CDs to Au3+ was further applied to monitor Enr. 300 μM Au3+ was firstly added in G-CDs solution and the quenched fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded. Then, Enr was added to the G-CDs/Au3+ solution and fluorescence emission spectrum was further recorded. For the selectivity test, possible interference including Pym, Kas, Osc, Car, Pro, Pyr, Cyr, LSM, Pho, Flo, Iso was added to replace Enr and the same assay procedure was performed.

Fig. 1 A displayed a representative TEM image of the obtained G-CDs. The morphology of G-CDs was mostly quasi-spherical with good dispersion. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1B presented obvious lattice fringes. The lattice fringe spacing was 0.21 nm, corresponding to (100) faces of graphite carbon [28]. Fig. 1C illustrated that the particle size of G-CDs was mostly within 3 nm. Moreover, FT-IR was used to determine the surface functional groups of G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, a typical absorption band at 3417 cm-1 was attributed to the O-H and N-H stretching vibration [29]. The peaks at 2917, 1627, 1400 and 1265 cm-1 were caused by the stretching vibration of C-H, CO, C-N and C-O-C [30,31], respectively. The above results demonstrated that -OH, -COOH and -NH2 existed on the surface of G-CDs, and nitrogen was successfully doped in the prepared G-CDs [32]. These functional groups not only improved the hydrophilicity and stability of G-CDs in aqueous solutions, but also interacted with certain target, which were conducive to broaden the application of G-CDs in detection. Furthermore, in order to further explore the structure and chemical bonds of G-CDs, XRD and XPS tests were carried out. Fig. 1E displayed a prominent wide peak near 25.04°, indicating that G-CDs was an amorphous graphite-like structure [26].

XPS survey spectrum depicted three characteristic peaks at about 285 eV, 399 eV and 531 eV, indicating that G-CDs mainly contain C, N and O, accounting for 79.44%, 9.63% and 10.93% respectively ( Fig. 2A). C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B) exhibited four characteristic peaks at 284.34 nm, 284.90 nm, 285.65 nm, and 288.56 nm, corresponding to C-C, C-N, C-O and CO, respectively [33,34]. N1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 2C illustrated the presence of pyridinic N at 399.4 eV and pyrrolic N at 400.5 eV [35]. O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D) indicated the existence of C-O (529.7 eV) and CO (531.2 eV) [31,36]. XPS results further confirmed the nitrogen doping of G-CDs and the formation of rich functional groups such as –OH, –NH2 and –COOH on the surface, which may be the main factors to improve and modulate the luminescence properties of G-CDs [37].

The optical properties of G-CDs were studied by UV and fluorescence spectra. Fig. 3A showed that the G-CDs has an obvious UV absorption peak at 241 nm and a weak shoulder peak at 300 nm, which was attributed to the π-π * transition of CC and the n-π * transition of CO, respectively [28]. Simultaneously, G-CDs presented an optimal emission wavelength of 520 nm excited at 390 nm. The synthesized G-CDs solution appeared brown-yellow under sunlight and green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Interestingly, the emission pattern of G-CDs displayed double emission peak at around 420 and 520 nm when excitation wavelength in the range of 300–350 nm, while single emission peak at about 520 nm when excitation wavelength in the range of 360–400 nm, as well as unobvious red shift phenomenon (Fig. 3B). Despite the existence of dual emission, G-CDs exhibited untypical excitation independent emission behaviour in a large wavelength range. This phenomenon also demonstrates a relatively uniform composition and structure of our G-CDs. Moreover, the coordinate corresponding to the maximum emission intensity was (0.23, 0.40) in the CIE chromaticity diagram, which further indicated the green fluorescence of G-CDs (Fig. 3C). The fluorescence quantum yield of as-fabricated G-CDs at room temperature was measured to be 19% using quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as reference and the detailed method was given in Supplementary information.

In order to further explore the properties of G-CDs, various stability tests were performed. After being stored at room temperature for more than 30 days, the reduction of relative fluorescence intensity was extremely limited (Fig. S1A). Likewise, G-CDs had good stability under high salt solution concentrations even up to 2 M NaCl (Fig. S1 B) and can also maintain stable after 2 h of continuous UV irradiation (light source: 450 W Xe lamp, 365 nm) (Fig. S1C). The good stability of G-CDs ensured the stability of fluorescence signal and the accuracy of testing results. It is noted that the initial prepared G-CDs was acidic (pH ≈4), which may be related to the abundant carboxyl groups on its surface. In Fig. S1D, its initial pH (≈4) of the G-CDs as control and the fluorescence intensity as F0. The fluorescence intensity of G-CDs would be strengthened if the pH was lower than 4, while weakened as the pH was higher than 4, and when the pH value was in the range of 6–13, it remained almost unchanged. The main reason for the sensitivity of G-CDs to pH may be due to the existence of many functional groups such as -NH2, -COOH on the surface of G-CDs [38]. Under different pH conditions, these functional groups underwent different degrees of protonation or deprotonation reactions. Remarkably, G-CDs was negatively charged (Zeta potential was −1.17 mV). Thus, the deprotonation of G-CDs gradually increased with the increase of pH, which further affected the energy band of the G-CDs owing to the conjugation change between functional groups and carbon structure [15,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The “on-off” fluorescent probe of G-CDs for detection of NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, G-CDs was employed to investigate the fluorescence response with different anions. Fig. 4A showed that only NO2 - had a significant quenching effect to G-CDs after adding different anions (NO2 -, HCO3 -, Br-, S2-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, SiO4 2-, MnO4 -, PO4 3-, HSO4 -, I-, Cl-, CO3 2-), while other anions provided relatively weak effect on fluorescence intensity of G-CDs. It can also be noted from the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs with and without anion, respectively) in Fig. 4B, indicating that the quenching response of G-CDs to NO2 - was evident and selective. Moreover, this fluorescence quenching behaviour of G-CDs to NO2 - could be observed clearly with naked eyes (Fig. 4C). It is worth noting that MnO4 - caused a darker fluorescence color to G-CDs in Fig. 4C, which may be due to its darker blue-violet color, but exhibited little effect on fluorescence intensity of G-CDs (Fig. 4B). The detection sensitivity to NO2 - was evaluated by adding varying amounts of NO2 - (0–300 μM). The results displayed the falling fluorescence intensity of G-CDs with the increase of NO2 - concentration, indicating that the degree of fluorescence quenching of G-CDs was positively correlated with the concentration of NO2 - (Fig. 4D). Fig. S2A plotted the relationship between quenching efficiency and NO2 - concentration. A linear response in the concentration range of 1–300 μM was found (R2 =0.9740). The calibration equation obtained was y = −0.0014x+ 0.8899, where y is quenching efficiency (F/F0) and x is the concentration of NO2 -. The limit of detection (LOD) to NO2 - was calculated to be 1.00 μM according to LOD= (3δ)/k, where k is the slope of the linear relationship curve, δ is blank standard deviation. 3 was taken for the detection limit calculation standard.

In addition, the interference of various anions on NO2 - was also studied. As shown in Fig. S2B, the coexistence of other anions displayed negligible effect on the quenching effect of NO2 -, which confirmed an excellent anti-disturbance ability. Compared with similar sensors reported in the literatures, our G-CDs displayed a comparable or even better performance ( Table 1). More importantly, G-CDs synthesized herein had many advantages, including bright green fluorescence, good water solubility and stability. On top of this, this method was simple, rapid, high selective and sensitive for the detection of NO2 -. Therefore, G-CDs can be used as a “on-off” fluorescence sensor for the qualitative and quantitative determination of NO2 -.

Up to now, although the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs was far from clear due to the abundance functional groups on CDs, which may be combined with the targets via different mechanisms, it was commonly divided into static quenching, dynamic quenching, energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), etc. [33,43]. In this work, the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-CDs induced by NO2 - was investigated by fluorescence lifetime, FT-IR and UV spectra. The fluorescence lifetime was usually hardly affected by static quenching and IFE, while easily altered by dynamic quenching and FRET [44]. The fluorescence lifetime after the addition of NO2 - was 7.33 ns, which was not much different from that of G-CDs (7.57 ns) ( Fig. 5A). Therefore, the almost negligible change in fluorescence lifetime excluded FRET between NO2 - and G-CDs. Furthermore, FT-IR spectra (Fig. 5B) of G-CDs before and after adding NO2 - showed light change, implying a weak combination between G-CDs and NO2 -. According to Fig. 5C, NO2 - had a wide UV absorption band centered at 360 nm, which partly overlapped with the excitation spectrum of G-CDs (390 nm), indicating that the quenching effect of NO2 - on G-CDs may be induced by an internal filtration effect. This resulted in irradiation shielding due to the competition of incident radiation between G-CDs fluorophore and NO2 - (quencher), leading to a great reduction in the fluorescence emission intensity of G-CDs [45,46]. Based on the above results, we confirmed that the main reasons for the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs by NO2 - are static quenching and IFE. Based on this, an “on-off” fluorescent probe of G-CDs for detecting NO2 was constructed as depicted in Fig. 5D.

In addition, the practicality of G-CDs was evaluated via standard addition experiments. When G-CDs were applied to the detection of NO2 - in actual tap water samples, the recoveries were between 90% and 118%, and the relative standard deviations were less than 3.9% ( Table 2), indicating that the prepared G-CDs was relatively reliable for practical testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for detection of Enr
ce:section.ce:para: Although G-CDs was simple and feasible as a sensor for monitoring certain substance such as NO2 - via fluorescence "on-off" processing, it was not sensitive to Enr. Therefore, we designed an "on-off-on" sensor to improve the sensitivity. After many experiments, Au3+ was selected as the quenching agent. Fig. 6A presented the relative fluorescence intensity of G-CDs before and after adding different metal ions (F/F0). Compared to other metal ions, the relative intensity of G-CDs with Au3+ addition was lower, indicating that Au3+ was an excellent quencher to be selected. It must be noted that Hg2+, Ag+, Fe3+ also had slight quenching response to G-CDs, but it was relatively weaker than Au3+ quenching effect. Moreover, the quenching response could be seen with naked eyes (Fig. 6B). Additionally, its quenching effect enhanced gradually (Fig. S3A) as the concentration of Au3+ increased (1–300 μM) and showed a good linear relationship (y = −0.0061x+0.8692, R2 =0.9893) ranging from 1 to 80 μM (Fig. S3B). Furthermore, the quenching behaviour of Au3+ to G-CDs had good anti-interference ability (Fig. S3C). these indicated that G-CDs could also be utilized to monitor Au3+.

Furthermore, the quenching mechanism of Au3+ to G-CDs was explored. Since G-CDs contained abundant reducing groups such as -NH2, -OH, which were able to reduce Au3+ to Au0, then formed Au nanoparticles (AuNPs). Therefore, we supposed that the quenching effect of G-CDs with the addition of Au3+ may be due to the formation of AuNPs. To verify the assumption, the mixed system of G-CDs+Au3+ was characterized with TEM, XRD and UV–vis. TEM image in Fig. 6C presented the formed nanoparticles, which were uniformly dispersed. HRTEM displayed clearly lattice stripes with the layer spacing 0.24 nm, which was equivalent to the lattice spacing of Au (111) (Fig. S4A) [47]. And the average particle size was in the range of 20–35 nm (Fig. S4B). In addition, Fig. 6D presented the XRD pattern, where the diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ = 38.1°, 44.3°, 64.5°, 77.6° and 81.6°. The peaks were in good agreement with JCPDS no. 44–0058 and attributed to (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) Bragg reflections of the face-centered cubic AuNPs structure, respectively [48]. Moreover, the UV spectrum presented a typical SPR absorption band (535 nm) of AuNPs and the mixed system appeared peculiar purple-red color of AuNPs under sunlight (Fig. 6E) [49]. These results confirmed that Au3+ was reduced by G-CDs to Au0, then formed AuNPs. We further analyzed the quenching behaviour of G-CDs after adding Au3+, the absorption band of the formed AuNPs greatly overlapped with the emission band of G-CDs (Fig. 6E), which could result in the decrease of G-CDs fluorescence intensity due to the nonradiative energy conversion. And the potential quenching effect was probably caused by FRET or IFE [41,47,48]. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs with and without Au3+ was 7.57 ns and 7.09 ns, respectively (Fig. 6F), meaning that the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs was decayed because of the formed AuNPs, proving the FRET between G-CDs and AuNPs rather than IFE.

Since the formed AuNPs had a significant quenching effect on G-CDs, based on this, G-CDs/AuNPs system as a sensor platform was further fabricated for the next tests and the selectivity was also investigated. Fig. 7A showed that only Enr caused a significant fluorescence restoration to G-CDs/AuNPs, while the effects of other potential substances were negligible. The relative fluorescence intensity of G-CDs/AuNPs after adding Enr was 25 times of other unresponsive reagents. Fig. 7B showed the emission spectra of G-CDs and G-CDs/AuNPs with Enr. Interestingly, the emission wavelength was shifted from the 520–440 nm. As shown in Fig. 7C, the fluorescence of G-CDs did not change significantly after adding Enr directly, meaning that G-CDs may do not interact with Enr [26]. However, after adding Enr to G-CDs/AuNPs system, the quenched fluorescence turned into a strong blue emission fluorescence. At the same time, the dispersion solution of G-CDs/AuNPs changed from purple-red to colorless with large black particles precipitated. The possible reason was that Enr broke the original equilibrium system and prompted the AuNPs to aggregated to large particles. In the meantime, G-CDs with larger particle size also aggregated with AuNPs, while the smaller particles of G-CDs still remained in dispersion solution. All these induced the destruction of FRET of AuNPs, as well as the fluorescence emission blue shifted. Additionally, it also may be because Enr possesses higher binding affinity through hydrogen bonding toward G-CDs than AuNPs, leading to the disappearance of stabilizing effect of G-CDs on AuNPs, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs and the fluorescence of G-CDs is restored (namely “off-on”). TEM image in Fig. 7D further confirmed that AuNPs formed larger size clusters, even larger size hexagons and equilateral triangles after adding Enr. The simulation of the entire reaction process was shown in Scheme 2.

Besides, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the increase of Enr concentration (Fig. S5A). Simultaneously, as displayed in Fig. S5B, there was a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9816) between the relative fluorescence intensity (F2/F1) and Enr concentration ranging from 1 to 50 mg/L, where F1＆F2 is the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs+Au3+ before and after adding Enr. The linear regression equation was y = 0.0707x+ 0.4910, where y is the relative fluorescence intensity (F2/F1) and x is the concentration of Enr. In addition, LOD to Enr was measured to be 0.04 mg/L based on 3δ/k, which was comparable or even lower than those of previously reported results ( Table 3). Moreover, this method had good selectivity, sensitivity and visibility, indicating the conducted G-CDs/AuNPs sensor platform could be used as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Enr.

The recovery rate of Enr in actual tap water samples was between 99.8% and 103.9%, and the relative standard deviation was less than 3.7%, indicating that the “on-off-on” sensor platform was relatively reliable and possessed potential in practical application of Enr detection ( Table 4).

In short, using chitosan and p-phenylenediamine as nitrogen and carbon sources, green fluorescence carbon dots (G-CDs) was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained G-CDs contained a variety of functional groups, displaying excellent water solubility, stability, and unobvious excitation-dependent behavior. Furthermore, a simple and rapid fluorescence “on-off” sensor was fabricated for the selective detection of NO2 - due to IFE of NO2 - to G-CDs. Moreover, a good linear relationship was obtained for NO2 - determination in the concentration ranging from 1 to 300 μM and an acceptable detection limit of 1 μM was achieved. Additionally, the fluorescence of G-CDs could also be quenched after adding Au3+, which may be attributed to FRET effect of the in-situ formed AuNPs to G-CDs. Consequently, the formed G-CDs/AuNPs system was further constructed as a sensor platform to detect Enr. The quenched fluorescence was recovered by Enr due to the addition of Enr led to the aggregation of AuNPs with some large size particles of G-CDs, and the fluorescence changed from green to blue. This "on-off-on" sensor method for detecting Enr possesses the advantages of high selectivity, sensitivity and visibility, and a good linear relationship was obtained at concentration ranging from 1 to 50 mg/L with a lower LOD (0.04 mg/L). More importantly, G-CDs was applied to determine NO2 - and Enr in actual water samples with acceptable reliability and accuracy, implying its potential value of sensor application.

Jiyao Xu: Experiment, analysis, Data collation, Writing – first draft, Experiment, Formal analysis. Quan Qi: Data sorting. Lili Sun: Sort out data and analyze. Xiangjun Guo: Data collation, Analysis, Software. Hongmei Zhang: Data collation, Analysis, Software. Xihui Zhao: Review and revise the article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41, No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.164519.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
